content:
Key Findings

  • Pendle Borough Council's total spending reached £129.1M across 49,741 transactions over five financial years.
  • The council engaged with 3,019 unique suppliers between 2021/22 and 2025/26.
  • Annual spending increased by £16.5M, an 85.5% rise, from 2021 to 2025.
  • The largest year-on-year increase in spending was £10.4M, a 41.0% rise, observed from 2024 to 2025.
  • Spending saw a notable increase of £6.6M, or 31.4%, from 2022 to 2023.

Overall Spending Trends at Pendle

Pendle Borough Council's financial activity shows a significant increase in expenditure over the past five financial years. From 2021/22 to 2025/26, the council spent a total of £129.1M. This substantial sum was distributed across 49,741 individual transactions, highlighting the breadth of the council's operations and its engagement with numerous service providers and vendors. The overall spending trend indicates a growing financial footprint for the council, with an increase of £16.5M, representing an 85.5% rise in annual expenditure when comparing 2021 to 2025.

The annual spending figures reveal a dynamic financial landscape. In 2021, the council spent £19.2M. This figure rose to £21.1M in 2022, marking a 9.7% increase. The most substantial year-on-year growth occurred between 2022 and 2023, with spending climbing by £6.6M to £27.7M, a 31.4% increase. While there was a slight dip in 2024, with spending decreasing by £-2,408,615 to £25.3M, the council's expenditure surged again in 2025 to £35.7M. This represents a significant £10.4M increase, or 41.0%, from the previous year.

These fluctuations suggest varying demands and priorities over the five-year period. The significant increase from 2024 to 2025, for example, points to either new large-scale projects, increased service delivery costs, or a combination of factors driving up expenditure. Understanding these trends is crucial for residents to assess the council's financial management and its impact on local services.

The Breadth of Supplier Engagement

Over the five financial years analysed, Pendle Borough Council engaged with a remarkable 3,019 unique suppliers. This high number of distinct vendors suggests a diverse procurement strategy, potentially involving a mix of long-term partners and numerous one-off or short-term engagements. The sheer volume of unique suppliers indicates that the council is not solely reliant on a small group of established contractors but actively seeks out a wide range of providers for its needs. This approach could foster competition and potentially lead to better value for money, but it also requires robust procurement processes to manage such a large supplier base effectively.

The distribution of £129.1M across 3,019 unique suppliers implies that while some suppliers may receive substantial contracts, many others are likely involved in smaller, more specific transactions. This pattern is common in local government, where councils procure a vast array of goods and services, from large infrastructure projects to everyday office supplies and specialist consultancy. The question of "new blood or old guard" becomes pertinent here, as a high number of unique suppliers could indicate a healthy influx of new businesses winning contracts, or it could reflect a high turnover of suppliers, with many only engaged for a single project or short period.

Fluctuations and Financial Shifts

The year-on-year spending changes at Pendle Borough Council highlight periods of both rapid expansion and slight contraction in expenditure. The most significant growth was observed from 2024 to 2025, with spending increasing by £10.4M, a 41.0% jump. This substantial rise could be attributed to major capital projects, increased service provision, or responses to evolving community needs. Similarly, the period from 2022 to 2023 saw a considerable increase of £6.6M, or 31.4%, reflecting another phase of heightened financial activity.

Conversely, the council experienced a decrease in spending of £-2,408,615, a reduction of -8.7%, from 2023 to 2024. Such fluctuations are part of managing public finances, often influenced by the completion of projects, changes in funding streams, or strategic reprioritisations. These shifts in annual budgets can impact supplier relationships, potentially leading to new contracts for emerging needs or the conclusion of agreements for completed programmes. Analysing these financial shifts helps to understand the underlying drivers of council spending and its implications for the local economy and the array of suppliers engaged.

What You Can Do

  • Submit a Freedom of Information request to Pendle Borough Council about their procurement policies for new suppliers or their average supplier retention rates.
  • Attend council committee meetings, which are open to the public, to hear discussions on financial decisions and contract awards.
  • Check the council's published contracts register to see details of ongoing and recently awarded contracts.
  • Write to your local councillor about your concerns regarding the council's spending patterns, the diversity of its supplier base, or how it ensures value for money from its contractors.
  • Visit aidoge.co.uk to explore the full spending data for Pendle Borough Council and other Lancashire councils yourself.
This article is based on Pendle Borough Council's published spending data covering 2021/22 to 2025/26. All figures are drawn directly from transactions published under the Local Government Transparency Code. Analysis by AI DOGE.
